title: Making a peat-free seed-raising compost
subtitle: Blending mature garden compost, coir, and horticultural grit for seed germination
excerpt: Commercial peat-free seed composts are often too coarse or too rich for fine seed germination. This is the mix for a fine-textured, low-fertility, free-draining seed compost using mature garden compost, coir fibre, and sharp horticultural grit, with the ratios and preparation steps needed to get consistent germination.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on seed composts and peat-free growing media. Garden Organic 'Making your own compost' factsheets. Charles Dowding growing guides on peat-free compost use. National Trust 'Peat-free update 2023' horticultural bulletin.

glossaryTerms.definition: Compressed coconut fibre, a byproduct of coconut processing. Used as a peat substitute in growing media. Coir has neutral pH, good moisture retention, and adequate aeration. It is low in nutrients, which is desirable for seed-raising mixes where high fertility causes problems.

A fine-textured, low-nutrient growing medium for germinating seeds. Seeds carry their own nutrient store for germination; a low-fertility compost avoids burning tender new roots. The fine texture ensures good seed-to-compost contact across the germination surface.

content.text: has three properties: fine texture for good contact with tiny seeds, low fertility to avoid burning new roots, and fast drainage so seeds do not sit in water. Mature garden compost alone is often too coarse and too nutrient-rich for seed sowing. Blending it with
and sharp grit adjusts all three properties.

Only use compost that has fully matured. Immature compost releases ammonia and soluble salts that damage seedlings. Properly finished compost smells earthy and looks dark brown throughout. Sieve through a 10 mm mesh to remove sticks, stones, and large lumps. The sieved material is the base of your blend.
For fine seeds (tomatoes, peppers, celery, Brassicas): 2 parts sieved compost, 2 parts coir, 1 part horticultural grit
For medium seeds (squash, courgettes, cucumbers, sweet corn): 3 parts sieved compost, 1 part coir, 1 part grit
For large seeds (beans, peas, nasturtiums): standard potting compost or blended compost without extra grit. Large seeds have enough nutrient reserve for richer soil.

Coir is sold as compressed bricks. Weigh the amount needed (each brick typically expands to 8 to 10 litres of rehydrated coir), pour over warm water at roughly 4 litres per brick, and leave for 30 minutes. Break the rehydrated coir apart with your hands, removing any large fibre clumps. The result should be fluffy and barely moist.
Combine the components in a trug or bucket and mix thoroughly. The finished mix should be slightly moist but not wet. Squeeze a handful: it should hold its shape briefly then crumble apart.
Fill seed trays or modules to within 5 mm of the rim. Firm lightly and level with a piece of board. Do not over-firm; the seedling needs to push its root through the compost.

Sow seeds at the recommended depth. Water from below by placing the tray in 10 mm of water for 10 minutes, then lifting out and allowing to drain. This avoids washing small seeds to one end of the tray.

Well-matured garden compost typically has a pH of 6.5 to 7.5, which suits most vegetable seedlings. Coir has a pH of approximately 5.8 to 6.8. The blend usually sits around 6.2 to 7.0 without adjustment. If sowing acid-loving plants (blueberries, heathers), use a higher proportion of coir and no compost, or buy a dedicated ericaceous growing medium.
